How To
Turn Ideas
into Dollars
A Special Report by David L. Deutsch
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 3
Ideas: Today’s Driving Force
Behind Your Business Success
Are ideas really a shortcut to riches?
Well, as a copywriter, marketing consultant and author, they’ve been responsible for putting a
small fortune into my bank account. And more than a billion dollars onto my clients’ bottom
lines.
I get paid for coming up with lots of ideas. But that’s because a single idea for a new product
or service can build a business. A single marketing idea can generate millions of dollars in
profits. An idea on how to save or make more money for a company can change a
career. Fortunes have even been built on single copy ideas!
Look around you—look at successful companies such as Amazon, eBay, FedEx and Microsoft.
What are they based on? Better ideas!
Ideas are the new currency of business. In The Rise of the Creative Class, Richard Florida
says that we’re almost all “creative workers” now and that…
Human creativity is the ultimate resource. The ability to come up with new
ideas and better ways of doing things is ultimately what raises productivity...
And consider this article from The New York Times:
Innovation is all the rage. Chief executives implore their troops to be
more creative, the editors of BusinessWeek are starting a new magazine devoted
to the subject, and Wall Street awards premiums to companies (like Apple
Computer) that come up with innovative products.
You can tell people to “think outside the box,” but that is not particularly
helpful. For one thing, as Robert Lutz, vice chairman of General Motors, has
pointed out, “being able to ‘think outside the box’ presupposes you were able to
think in it.”
I’d like to propose 10 simple words that can have a profound impact on your life…
Ideas are the key to the financial success you desire.
And conversely…
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 4
A lack of ideas can prevent you from achieving the financial success you desire.
If you can come up with ideas, if you have imagination, you will never want for money
or success. For example, in How to Use Your Imagination to Make Money, James D. Woolf and
Charles B. Roth describe a man who used ideas to overcome some pretty tough circumstances.
George Livingston Baker had a secure job with the State Highway Department. But an
economic downturn hit, and he soon found himself out of a job.
But he wasn’t out of ideas. Far from it, in fact—he was positively bursting with them!
So he decided to use his newfound freedom to do what he always wanted to do: Head for
the mountains, find a secluded place and be a pioneer for profit. Before long, he had to pause his
idea-generation tactics, because he was having to turn business away so as not to lose the leisure
time for which he had gone to the mountains in the first place!
How did he do it? How did he earn money so far from where most people lived? He didn’t do it
in just one single way. He actually had 36 different money-making methods!
For example…
• He drove tourists up and down the mountain. (Many were scared to drive the
winding roads, and he knew all the best routes and where to find all the best views.)
• He guided fishermen and hunters to the best fishing and hunting spots.
• In the winter, he made rustic furniture from the pine trees and sold it to visitors.
(This alone was enough to support him, even if he had earned no other income.)
• He made a cold remedy from mountain herbs and sold it.
• He found a plant that killed tobacco cravings and sold it by mail order.
• He made money by tanning the hides from the sportsmen he guided and mounting
the animals’ heads.
• He got a job as a local correspondent for the county paper.
• And he made money in 29 other ways as well!
After six years, he had amassed a small fortune (guess he didn’t believe in economic downturns),
six years of wonderful memories and the knowledge that he could always make a good living
doing exactly what he wanted to do.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 5
If George Baker could use ideas to make much more money than he needed in the middle of
nowhere during an economic downturn, surely you too can tap into the power of ideas to make
your financial dreams come true.
And in this Special Report, you’re going to learn how.
A NOTE ON THINKING INSIDE THE BOX
In my Idea Power course, I show how to think inside 15 boxes that help you generate ideas—the
quickest way I’ve ever found to become a creative genius.
These are the “boxes” you’ll see referred to throughout this report.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 6
If You Have (or Want To Have)
Your Own Business
Business cannot exist without ideas.
They are the sparks that start new businesses and the fuel that keeps them running and profitable.
FedEx started as an idea for guaranteed overnight delivery. Microsoft started as an idea for a
computer operating system. Amazon started as an idea to sell books online and evolved into a
convenient way to shop for every gift, game and household need you can imagine. eBay was
simply an idea for an easy way to bid for items over the internet.
Then, after they’re started, the competitive and profit-making strength of a business is simply
the power and quality and quantity of their ideas—for better products and services, for delivering
their products and services and for selling and promoting their products and services.
Here’s how to use ideas to grow (or start) your own business…
1. Build a business with an idea: Sometimes, it only takes a single product idea to start a
business. With a great idea you can even sell rocks—like the Pet Rock (just a rock in a box
with clever packaging and a tongue-in-cheek “Care and Feeding” booklet). Amazon, eBay,
Uber… all started as ideas.
Why, it’s so easy to invent a moneymaking product, even a child can do it! In fact…
• A few years ago, two young farm boys named Coy Funk and Skylar Schipper
raised money for their school by selling cow patties from their pasture to neighbors who
needed fertilizer for their gardens and lawns. They playfully called their product “Moo
Poo.” Today these youngsters’ business, Manure Gourmet, takes in gross sales of
$20,000 a year and includes “Cow Wow,” “Llama Beans,” and “Billy (Goat) Chili.” Talk
about turning lemons into lemonade! (See Box #8, Convert into a Benefit.)
• In the 1940s, a 14-year-old boy named Philo Farnsworth invented the “scan lines” that
make television possible—by observing the rows of his family’s plowed field. (See Box
#15, Change the Look.)
• The snowboard was invented by Tom Sims, an 8th grader from New Jersey, in
1963. (See Box #14, Change the Shape.)
• 14-year-old Chester Greenwood invented earmuffs, patented his invention and built his
own factory to make them when he grew up. (See Box #3, Divide It Up.)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 7
• Frank Epperson was just 11 when he invented the popsicle. Before long, he was selling
them all over the country. (See Box #14, Change the Shape.)
• Do you love to water ski? You can thank a boy named Ralph Samuelson, who invented
the sport in the 1920s. (See Box #5, Bring Things Together.)
• And recently, Richie Stachowski, all of 11 years old, created a device that lets people talk
underwater by turning a snorkel into a megaphone. A major retailer ordered 50,000 of his
“Water Talkies”! Then, he invented a “Scuba Scope” that lets snorkelers see under and
above the water at the same time! (See Box #1, Add a New Dimension.)
Here’s my point: If these guys can do it, surely you should be able to look around, find
a need and come up with a product that can fill it! Then you can either license it, sell
it yourself in stores, online, or through direct mail or sell the idea to someone else and let
them manufacture and sell it.
Of course, you don’t have to invent a tangible product. You can come up with a
unique service, such as the “kid taxis” from a few years ago to give moms a break
from chauffeuring kids around. Or you could create a downloadable product: a special report,
a book, a video or a course.
Look around and see what people want and need, what they’re searching for, what they
complain about and find a way to help them and solve their problems by using the boxes.
2. Create a new product or service: If you already have a business, you have customers who
already trust you. You have channels to communicate with and sell to them easily and at a
reasonable cost. So use the boxes in Idea Power to create additional products or services you
can sell them. (For example, Box #3, Divide Something, might inspire you to subdivide your
market and create special products or services for each segment.)
3. Take an existing product or service and make it better: FedEx didn’t invent package
delivery. Fred Smith took an existing idea (dependable package delivery) and made it better
(overnight, guaranteed package delivery) by using Box #9, Switch Gears (from slow, to fast
and dependable).
4. Find a better way to deliver your product or service: Ray Kroc didn’t invent hamburgers
and French fries. But his McDonald’s restaurants sure were a better idea for delivering them
to us—faster and cheaper than anywhere else. And until Jeff Bezos had the idea for Amazon,
who would have thought there was a better idea out there for how people could order and
receive books that is, in many ways, even better than an in-person bookstore?
5. Become a high-paid consultant: Not only is corporate America starving for more and better
ideas, but 22 million business owners are desperate for more and better ideas to start and grow
their businesses, deal with a rapidly changing marketplace and for coming up with new
products and services.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 8
Don’t know how to start? Start small. Start part-time. Find a company willing to give you a
try. If you have to, for free at first. Or work “on spec” (if they’re not happy, they don’t pay).
Alternatively, work only for a percentage of the money you make (or save) them. Even a
small percentage can add up to big income for you in time.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 9
Ideas for More Sales
What really makes someone effective at selling?
Study top salespeople and you’ll find they have better ideas. Better ideas for getting in to see
prospects. Better ideas for how prospects can use their goods or services. Better ideas
for communicating to prospects and overcoming objections. And better ideas for standing out
from everyone else trying to sell those prospects.
For example:
1. Stand out to be outstanding: Why should you do what every other salesperson does? Use
Box #2 and Do the Opposite. For example, stand out from the crowd by asking
for appointments at 10 minutes after the hour—rather than on the hour or half hour,
as everyone else does.
2. Come up with better ways customers can use your product or service: The more you can
show prospects new and creative ways to use your products and services, the more you’ll sell.
So try using every one of the 15 boxes to explore different possibilities.
3. Create openings to get in the door: Let’s take, for example, Box #6, Put One Thing Inside
Another. Instead of trying to get an appointment by phone or email, put your request inside
something else and send it to your prospect. Maybe that means taping your message inside the
cover of a nice hot pizza that you have delivered a little before lunch one day. Or on a Post-it
Note attached to a book you think they would enjoy reading.
4. Demolish Objections: You can use these idea boxes to light a fire under any aspect of your
sales presentation. But they’re especially powerful for overcoming objections. For example,
the price objection. You could use Box #3 and divide the price (“$1,000 amounts to just $3 a
day.”), Box #8 to convert it into a benefit (“The high price means most of your competitors
won’t be able to afford this machine—and you’ll have a unique competitive advantage!”),
Box #15 to change the look (“This home study course is inexpensive compared to attending a
seminar: airfare, hotel, time away from work and the high price of the seminar itself.”), and so
on.
5. Get Creative with Pricing: Being creative with pricing gives you a tremendous advantage
when selling anything. When a prospect thinks the price is too high, for example, it’s often
possible to divide it into installments, or to package up the product or services in creative
ways that add more perceived value to the prospect.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 10
How Ideas Can Create More Response
In Your Advertising and Promotions
Creativity has given me a huge advantage when it comes to producing extremely effective
advertising, direct mail and internet promotions for myself and my clients, which have brought
in over a billion dollars in sales over the years.
More often than not, creativity is what has enabled my promotions to outperform the promotions
of other writers (when tested head-to-head). Some other writers may be able to write just as well
and just as persuasively but lack the added turbo-boost that comes from pushing your creativity
to its limits.
Creativity can supercharge your persuasion in many different ways. Here are three of the
most important:
1. Saying the right thing: Often the power of a great advertisement, direct mail letter or other
promotion lies not so much in how it is written as the power of the message it is built on. In
other words, it’s not how it’s said, but what is said—the underlying message, the positioning,
the Unique Selling Proposition, and so on.
For example, most health prospects for health newsletters are bombarded with
messages telling them they should eat right, exercise, drink water and so on. So a few years
ago, a promotion for a health newsletter by Arthur Johnson did something shockingly
different and unexpected. His promotion asked, “Had Enough?” and told us many of the foods
we think are good for us aren’t, that exercise can be hazardous to your health and
that drinking more water may not do any good whatsoever.
It generated a record-breaking number of new subscribers for the newsletter—simply
by creating an unexpected, even shocking, message. (Box #2, Do the Opposite, can help you
find ways to put this creative technique to work for yourself.)
When you create a promotion, try running this process through each of the 15 boxes. For
example, Box #4, Take Something Out, could inspire you to make your basic message
stronger by removing extraneous promises and paring it down to a single strong message. Few
things help a message get through as well as sharpening it to a fine point.
2. Getting attention: You can’t save souls in an empty church. You’ve got to get them in the
door—of your church, or, metaphorically speaking, your advertisement, mailing piece or
website.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 11
People are drawn to that which is creative because creative ideas are new,
unexpected, interesting and exciting. (Just don’t be like a Madison Avenue ad agency and
become so focused on creativity that you lose sight of the fact that you’re supposed to be
selling something.)
I often use Box #5, Bring Some Things Together, when I want to make something more
captivating. For example, I’ll often combine a headline with a well-known phrase, book title
or movie title. “Dirty Rotten Medical Scoundrels” was one I used for a promotion about
doctors’ medical misdeeds. And for a new cancer treatment: “Cancer’s Last Stand.”
Try running this angle through each of the 15 boxes and see what sort of can’t-be-ignored
message or proposition you will find for your promotion.
3. Making your message more convincing: For a book on household hints, I wanted to feature
a trick for stretching too-tight shoes. Believe it or not, you put them in the freezer! Just put a
tightly sealed bag of water in each shoe. The water freezes, expands, and gently stretches the
shoes.
For the headline, I could have written: “How to fix too-tight shoes—by putting them in the
freezer.” That’s not a bad headline. But running it through Box #13, Add Something to the
Interaction (such as an intermediary), helped me come up with an idea for a great headline:
“Honey, why are my new shoes in the freezer?!”
Turning it into a first-person story made it much more convincing and compelling—because it
became a story instead of just a statement.
Run this concept through the 15 boxes as well. For example, Box #12, Change the
Interaction’s Intensity, could inspire you to get your reader angry about something—perhaps
that he is being somehow kept unfairly from getting the benefits of your product or service.
Few things convince a person you’re on their side more quickly than sharing their anger with
them.
On a more micro level, take as many components of your promotion as you can—your
headlines, subheads, bullets, paragraphs, lines of copy you want to improve—and run them
through the 15 boxes to see where the questions take you.
For example, say you’re promoting a health article telling men that their back pain is often
caused by a fat wallet in their back pocket throwing their back out of whack while they sit. You
could write an ordinary teaser for that idea like: “Why your wallet could be causing your back
pain.” Or, you could see Box #8, Convert (a Negative) into a Benefit. That box might make you
think about negative things associated with wallets, such as pickpockets. Which, when converted
into a benefit becomes: “How a pickpocket can cure your back pain.”
That’s how you get to great copy!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 12
(Of course, it also helps to have my new course, A-List Copywriting Secrets.)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 13
Recycle Your Ideas
For Extra Income!
You can make a lot of money with an idea.
But you can make even more if you recycle your ideas over and over again.
Let’s say you come up with an idea that generates a lot of profits for yourself, your client or your
employer by generating more sales or by reducing expenses. Maybe it’s a better way to
advertise, a better sales approach or a new operational technique. Maybe it’s a new ad campaign.
Maybe it’s an entire system for marketing, production or some other aspect of the business.
Why not make money for yourself or your company by selling that idea or system to…
• Similar businesses outside your geographic area (if it works for your pet store
in Albuquerque, why shouldn’t it work for a pet store in Des Moines?).
• Non-competitive businesses in your own geographic area who could benefit from it (what
works for a jewelry store may also be very effective for a high-end clothing store).
• Other individuals (salespeople, executives, etc.) who could benefit from it, if it
enhances their personal productivity or earning power.
How do you sell your ideas to others?
1. Sell the ideas outright (you give me money, then I give you the idea, technique,
or system).
2. Write and sell a book, report or program (a book or report with other components,
such as a workbook and video lessons).
3. License the ideas or techniques.
4. Create a seminar or an online course.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 14
5. Offer your ideas or techniques for a percentage of the increased profits (people love
no-risk offers like this!).
6. Coach people individually or in groups (in person or by phone).
One last thing: Why limit yourself to making money by recycling your own ideas? Why
not recycle other people’s ideas as well?
Now that you know the money-making potential of ideas, you can approach other individuals
or companies who have a better way of doing something and offer to market their ideas for them.
You could compensate them for the idea with a straight fee, a licensing arrangement,
a percentage of the sales revenue you generate or in any other way that’s agreeable to both
parties.
I personally know of many people who have made small fortunes taking their own ideas—
or those of others—and selling them over and over again.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 15
Job Hunting?
Look for Dirty Windows!
When Edward Bok was a young man with a sick mother to care for, he needed a job—
and he needed it fast.
While looking (hungrily) at a display window of a bakery, the owner came out and asked
him how the cakes and pies looked.
“They would look great,” Bok replied, “with a really clean window.” And as you can probably
guess, young Bok got himself a job.
You probably don’t want to wash windows. But there’s an important principle here:
Don’t just look for a job.
Look for dirty windows—that is, look for needs you can fill.
Employers don’t want employees per se. They want to have their needs filled. They have jobs to
be done, problems to be solved and profits to be made.
Use your creativity skills and Idea Power to generate ideas for what the needs of potential
employers might be, and to come up with ideas to fill them.
Here are some ways job hunters can use the power of ideas to boost their search:
1. Be a problem-solver and opportunity exploiter: Come up with ways you can help the
company solve problems—or take advantage of opportunities. How? By finding out
everything you can about the company—reading everything you can get your hands on and
talking to everyone who will talk to you. Then, use any or all of the boxes to find many ways
you can solve their problems or help exploit the opportunities.
2. Stand out from the crowd: Use the boxes to help you stand out from other applicants. For
example, consider Box #1, which talks about adding a sensory dimension. Can you add a
website to your resume with a video that introduces you? Should you send a tasty treat along
with your thank you note?
3. Change the interaction: Box #13, Add Something to the Interaction, is particularly important
to job hunters. Do everything you can, for example, to come to a company’s attention
endorsed by someone the company trusts—a current or former employee, for example, or
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 16
someone they respect in the industry. Such an endorsement changes the interaction between
prospective employee and employer entirely.
4. How to get paid more: Use ideas to make sure you get the compensation you want
and deserve. For example, let’s say they’re not willing to pay you the salary you feel you
deserve. Use Box #2 and Do the Opposite! You might ask for (or accept) less salary—but
with a very lucrative performance-based bonus that ensures that you will make more than you
possibly could have on the salary they were offering.
5. Take a lesson from P.T. Barnum: Everybody loves good showmanship. Use the boxes to
come up with ideas for impressing potential employers. (But do it without going over the top,
lest your showmanship come across as “showoffmanship.”)
If you’re sending samples of your work or other materials to a prospective employer, why not
show them you know a little about presentation and showmanship? You might use Box #1
(Add a New Dimension) to add a nice look and feel (put them into a leather binder) or sound
(include a website with a video).
Or, say you’re on the East Coast and a hot high-tech company in California expresses an
interest in you. Use Box #9 to Switch Gears and go overboard! Instead of arranging a Zoom
call or coming in for an interview in a few days or weeks, shift into high gear and say: “How
about if I meet you tomorrow for lunch?” Then book yourself on the first plane out. (It’s three
hours earlier in California, so you can easily make lunch even if you’re coming from the
East.)
Of course, if you want to get ahead and make more money, your creativity shouldn’t stop
when you get hired. In fact, it should blossom! And that’s why the next section can mean the
difference between a good and a great career.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 17
When You Work
For Someone Else
Picture a day in the life of a company supervisor. Two of his direct reports are scheduled for
their annual reviews.
The first one comes in and says, “I’ve worked hard the past year. I’ve never been late and
always did what I was told.”
The second one comes in and says, “I gave you an idea to cut costs that will save us $100,000
this year. And my idea for a new product is going to bring in $500,000 in additional profits.”
So… which one do you think is going to get the biggest raise?
If you work for someone else, don’t envy entrepreneurs who are free to pursue a wide range
of moneymaking paths and make as much money as possible—unconstrained by
corporate compensation policies. Instead, use them as inspiration to become an entrepreneur
inside the company you work for—an intrapreneur!—and use your company’s vast resources to
make more money for them (and yourself).
How? With ideas of course…
1. Come up with new ideas for products or services: Find new ways for the company to make
money. Create a new product or line of products. Be a profit center for your company—not
just an expense. All the boxes are great for this. But why not start with the Slice and Dice
boxes: Do the Opposite, Divide Something Up, Take Something Out and Bring Some Things
Together, and see how you can mix and match and combine existing products or services in
new moneymaking ways.
2. Come up with ways to save the company money: Helping a company spend less money is
better than helping them make more money. Why? If you make the company you work for a
dollar, they pay up to 50% taxes on it, so they only make 50 cents. But if you save them a
dollar, that’s a full untaxed dollar that goes right to their bottom line.
Try using Box #7, Do It Beforehand, to see what can be done in advance to save money down
the road. Or Box #4, Take Something Out, to see what’s unnecessary that could be
eliminated.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 18
3. Come up with better ways to market or promote your company’s products or services:
Every for-profit company needs better sales ideas, better advertising or promotional ideas and
better ways to communicate what the company offers to those who buy its products or use its
services. And nonprofits need ideas for raising money and communicating with their many
target markets.
Any of the boxes can trigger great marketing, sales or advertising ideas. But be especially sure
to check out the Right Stuff boxes, which all relate to writing and advertising.
4. Keep careful track of your results: Be sure to keep a record of any and all quantifiable
improvements that come about because of your ideas. Use this record to justify promotions
and salary increases. Don’t expect others in the company to keep track and remember what
you’ve accomplished—toot your own horn. Squeaky wheels get the grease.
5. Set up a separate business within the company: If the company you work for is reluctant to
implement your money-making ideas, offer to head up a separate business unit to market your
product or service yourself. Take some of the risk along with them. Become a true
intrapreneur.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 20 --

How to Make More Money with Ideas ©Deutsch & Associates, Inc. Page 19
What To Do NOW
Here’s a hard truth…
Making money with ideas is quite challenging…
IF you don’t take the time to come up with great ideas and make the effort to put these ideas to
work for you.
So here’s what I would recommend…
1. Take the time: Set aside just a few minutes a day to come up with new ideas. If you want to
start with just a minute or two today, that’s fine. Small improvements yield big results.
2. Write it down: How many truly great ideas have vanished into thin air because someone
thought, “I’ll remember that”? Keep a pad and pen with you at all times to write down your
ideas. Keep one by your bed while you sleep. Recording apps are great for capturing ideas,
too—and much safer than writing if you’re driving.
3. Have a quota: If you’re an employer, insist your employees come to meetings with at least
three ideas. And reward them handsomely for the ones you implement. If you’re an
employee or self-employed, give yourself a quota—for example, at least one good idea
every day. James Altucher suggests coming up with 10 ideas a day every day for yourself
and for others. It has transformed his life and the lives of many others.
4. Take a hard look: When you’ve got a nice selection of ideas, take a good look at them.
Analyze the costs versus the benefits of implementing them. Then decide which ones to put
into practice.
5. Take action: Last but by no means least, ACT! Nothing happens without action. And more
is learned from implementation than contemplation. More money is made from it, too.
I’d love to hear how you’re putting ideas to work in your business and personal life. I can be
reached at david@DavidLDeutsch.com.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 20 --
